rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical asymmetric Ni-catalyzed three-component alkoxy-alkylation of alkenes with 2-acylimidazoles and methanol, forming β-methoxy ketones with a quaternary stereocenter via radical addition.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of the Ni(II)-bound enolate (derived from 2-acylimidazole 1a deprotonated by quinuclidine/2,6-di-tert-butylpyridine) generates a Ni-stabilized α-carbonyl radical that adds to the alkene; the resulting benzylic radical/cation is trapped by methanol.

key_intermediates.name: benzylic radical from radical addition to 1,1-diphenylethylene (2a)
ring-opened homoallyl/allyl-carbinyl radical from cyclopropane probe 19

electron_flow: The Ni(II)/L8 complex binds the deprotonated 2-acylimidazole; anodic oxidation of this Ni-enolate produces a Ni-stabilized α-carbonyl radical that adds to the alkene to give a benzylic radical, which is further oxidized to a benzylic cation and trapped by MeOH, while protons are reduced at the Pt cathode.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Product 3a not formed; TEMPO-adduct of the α-carbonyl carbon isolated in 99% yield, supporting an α-carbonyl radical intermediate.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO+ BF4- (oxoammonium)
probe_reagents.observation: In the absence of current, TEMPO+ alone yields the same TEMPO-adduct (76%, 59% ee), indicating that TEMPO+ generated in situ by anodic oxidation could also be the trapping species; cannot rule out TEMPO oxidation pathway.

probe_reagents.name: (1-phenyl-2-phenylcyclopropyl)ethylene 19 (radical clock)
probe_reagents.observation: Ring-opened products 20 (13%, d.r. 1:1) and 21 (10%, d.r. 1:1) observed, consistent with a carbon-centered radical that opens the cyclopropane (intermediate [20-A] → [20-B]).

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of NiCl2-L8 + 1a vs TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Onset potential of the Ni-bound enolate is ~+0.50 V vs SCE, similar to TEMPO, supporting feasibility of anodic generation of the Ni-enolate radical.

notes: Constant-current (2-6 mA) and constant-potential (+1.0 V vs SCE) experiments both give 3a with consistently high ee (92-93%), indicating the stereo-determining step is Ni/L8-controlled radical addition rather than potential-dependent. Authors explicitly acknowledge an alternative pathway in which TEMPO is first oxidized to TEMPO+ which then traps the radical/enolate; this cannot be excluded based on the CV and stoichiometric TEMPO+ experiment.
